rto_tiers: Allowable-downtime tiers per critical mission function: 0-24h / 0-48h / 24-48h / 24-72h / 48+h, plus priority High/Medium/Low; per-function MAD and RTO in worksheets (e.g., Finance MAD 40 hours, RTO 24-40 hours; Academic Affairs RTO 24-72 hours)
vital_records_approach: Databases backed up nightly/weekly with copies stored off-site (bank vault safe-deposit box holds DRP, backup media, administrator info); manual checks held in Administrative Services vault; functional backup rather than a labeled vital-records program
tte_cadence: States BCP 'is exercised and reviewed annually'; some work-around procedures marked tested; no structured TT&E program, exercise types, training plan, or after-action/improvement cycle described
reconstitution_treatment: Not addressed as a distinct return-to-normal process; recovery framed per-function
academic_continuity_treatment: Strong: Academic Affairs worksheet pivots instruction to Blackboard Ultra online platform (all courses have master online shells, faculty trained), relocation to nearest campus or external/local-school sites; MAD 24-72h tied to TCSG instructional contact-hour standards
it_continuity_treatment: Detailed IT Disaster Recovery Plan covering 13+ systems (telephone, network, PeopleSoft, Banner, Active Directory, email, web, document imaging, etc.) with pre-disaster protection (nightly/weekly off-site backups, bank-vault storage), post-disaster recovery steps, RTO priorities and cost/time estimates
interdependencies_treatment: Per-function vendor/utility/contractor dependencies identified (Parker Fiber, AT&T/BellSouth, GTA, Georgia Power, banks), reliance on TCSG and other technical colleges for mutual aid and alternate sites

unusual_decisions.decision: Plan is built entirely as per-division fill-in worksheets submitted annually to the state system office rather than a single narrative COOP
unusual_decisions.quote: Once the Committees' review is complete the Plan is reviewed by the College President and submitted to the Emergency Manager at TCSG by May 1 st of each year.

unusual_decisions.decision: Cashiering/finance continuity drops to hand-written receipts reconciled in Excel until Banner is restored
unusual_decisions.quote: If Banner is unavailable, a hand-written receipt will be given to the student and the receipt number will be recorded on the excel spreadsheet. Student detail information will be recorded and reconciled every day manually in an excel spreadsheet until the system is restored.

unusual_decisions.decision: Operating bank account kept accessible to three signatories so any one can act if the others are unavailable
unusual_decisions.quote: We would have to make sure that our operating account remained fully accessible to at least three different people with security to make decisions in the absence of one or both of the other two.

analyst_notes: Read all 30 pages. ATC's 2025-2026 BCP is a state-mandated (TCSG Policy 3.4.1) departmental-worksheet plan: signed promulgation page, Critical Mission Functions chart with allowable-downtime tiers, a Hazard Vulnerability Assessment, per-division recovery worksheets (Finance, Economic Development, IT, Student Affairs, Academic Affairs), an embedded IT-DRP, and emergency contacts. Strongest on academic continuity (online pivot) and IT-DR; absent are succession orders, delegations of authority, a four-phase CONOPS, devolution, reconstitution-as-process, recognized continuity-doctrine citations, budgeting, and a structured TT&E program.

essential_functions: Public Safety (emergency response, dispatch, EOC activation)
rto_tiers: Per-process 'Allowable Downtime' (e.g., None/0-1 day/½ day/1-2 days/2-3 days/5 days/peak vs off-peak) plus 'Priority for Recovery' High/Medium/Low across all divisions
vital_records_approach: Unit plans must 'provide for the ongoing back up of critical data and protection of critical equipment' and 'store at least one current copy in an off-site facility with immediate availability'; DIT runs a separate Disaster Recovery Plan for core systems
tte_cadence: Unit BCPs must be exercised at least once every two years (objectives, validation, documented results, corrections); annual unit-head review; training for staff and within 120 days for new employees
reconstitution_treatment: Unit plans must 'establish procedures for return to full, normal operations of the operating unit, including that of non-critical functions'
academic_continuity_treatment: 'Learning and Teaching' is one of three critical-process categories (lecture & teaching, research, continuing education, graduation, student support services); college/library/academic-unit recovery deferred to college-level plans
it_continuity_treatment: Division of Information Technology 'maintains a separate Disaster Recovery Plan that addresses the recovery and the continuity of those services'; DIT core services treated as basic to recovery of all other processes
interdependencies_treatment: BIA explicitly built on 'interdependencies between these processes'; unit plans must ensure depended-on departments and external suppliers also have continuity plans; risk analysis extracted from the campus Disaster Recovery Plan

unusual_decisions.decision: The university-wide document plus all collected unit plans together constitute the complete BCP, with the VP for Student Affairs as compiler
unusual_decisions.quote: The Vice President for Student Affairs will be responsible for collecting all unit plans and the combination of this document and the unit plans will constitute the University's complete business Continuity Plan.

unusual_decisions.decision: Cross-unit prioritization during activation is reserved to the President's Cabinet after the fact rather than pre-scripted
unusual_decisions.quote: In the event plan activation requires prioritization among units for the recovery of services or allocation of limited resources, that prioritization will be accomplished by the President's Cabinet after consideration of the exact circumstances surrounding the plan activation.

unusual_decisions.decision: Activation authority is tiered: unit managers for local events, Cabinet for university-wide events
unusual_decisions.quote: The determination that an interruption has occurred may be made by the individual unit manager for local occurrences or by the Cabinet for university-wide occurrences.

analyst_notes: Read all 14 pages. BSU's BCP is a university-wide, division-by-division plan: a detailed BIA inventory across every division with allowable-downtime and recovery priority, a risk assessment and hazard matrix, a continuity-management process figure (pre-incident planning / incident response / maintenance), a 'Policy' section assigning continuity authority to division EPP committees and the President's Cabinet, and unit-plan requirements covering alternate sites, off-site data backup, activation triggers, and return-to-normal procedures. IT-DR is delegated to a standalone DIT plan. Missing: recognized continuity-doctrine citations, budgeting, orders of succession, delegations of authority, resilient/interoperable continuity communications, and devolution.

rto_tiers: Operational capability within 12 hours of activation, sustained up to 30 days (90 days for pandemic flu); implementation timeline appendix sequences actions at 0:00/2:00/4:00/8:00 hours and within 1 day / 3 days; 'business disruption' trigger = loss of an instructional component or any critical function for 2 consecutive instructional days or 3 consecutive calendar days
succession_depth: Two named alternates deep per key position (Appendix A), plus a Cabinet succession line; President -> Alternate 1 -> Alternate 2 with departmental successions
vital_records_approach: Off-site 'GO KIT' with hardcopy Business Process Guides, vital-records list and computer access pathways, essential electronic files on flash/CD; PeopleSoft vital records on Salt Lake City server with annual Chancellor's Office backup exercise; facilities blueprints/as-builts on-site
tte_cadence: Every business unit performs at least one tabletop exercise per year (each sub-unit within 7 years); not less than every seventh year a full-scale mock exercise from emergency response through BCP; FEMA IS-546/547/548/520/522 courses recommended; after-action reports within 30/90 days
devolution_treatment: Listed as a consolidated continuity element and named as an operational endpoint ('sustained operations for up to 30 days... or until devolution'), though devolution mechanics are lighter than the named alternate-site/relocation procedures
reconstitution_treatment: Dedicated 'How Will CSUB Return to Normal?' section: BCP IC plus Chief of UPD and Response Team determine completion; President in collaboration with Cabinet sets conditions for resuming normal operations; after-action report within 30 days
academic_continuity_treatment: Class relocation, modified syllabi/schedules, web-based classes, Deans Council convened for alternate teaching protocols; Class 3 (communicable disease) recovery includes alternate instruction delivery; Provost confers with Chancellor's Office and faculty on course delivery and academic credit
it_continuity_treatment: Separate IT Recovery Plan and Kuali plan referenced throughout; clustered servers/redundant VMs, contingent contracts with CENIC/Dell/Verizon/PBX vendors, Data Center Disaster Recovery Plan, automatic Chancellor's Office web failover, mobile trailer/terminal reconnection of the My CSUB portal within 24 hours
interdependencies_treatment: Three incident classes scaled by impact on interdependencies; contingent vendor contracts enumerated; mutual aid and cooperative agreements with other CSU campuses (e.g., payroll input at CSU Fresno) and local agencies/hospitals

unusual_decisions.decision: Explicitly maps the plan to the consolidated COOP/COG continuity-program elements drawn from the Cal EMA template
unusual_decisions.quote: A viable Business Continuity Plan identifies essential functions and consists of plans and procedures, alternate facilities, interoperable communications, data support systems, and is reinforced by comprehensive training, orientation, and exercise programs.

unusual_decisions.decision: Pre-positions contingent contracts for tents, water, generators, sanitation, food and trash so the campus can stand up a temporary tented operating site for up to 30 days
unusual_decisions.quote: Contingent contracts (vendors listed below) may be utilized during an event to procure items such as temporary tents, sanitation facilities, water, food, fencing, generators, trash removal, and computers. These contracts provide the campus with the ability to sustain operations for a period of up to 30 days.

unusual_decisions.decision: Defines a precise quantitative activation trigger tied to instructional/critical-function downtime
unusual_decisions.quote: A "business disruption" is defined as the loss or anticipated loss of a) any instructional component, or b) any critical function for two consecutive instructional days or three consecutive calendar days.

analyst_notes: Read all 48 pages. CSUB's BCP is the most complete plan in this batch, explicitly structured around the consolidated continuity-of-operations/continuity-of-government elements from the Cal EMA template and authorized under CSU EO 1014, EO 921, HSPD-20/NSPD-51, and NIMS. It covers all 22 benchmark components: signed-policy authority, three-incident-class CONOPS, BIA/risk assessment, essential functions with 12h/30d/90d objectives, two-deep succession (Appendix A) and a delegations-of-authority table with triggers/limits, multiple alternate-facility models (reciprocal CSU campuses, tented sites, telework), off-site GO-KIT vital records, a standalone IT Recovery/Data Center DR plan, academic continuity, devolution, a return-to-normal reconstitution section, and a tiered TT&E program (annual tabletops, septennial full-scale mock, FEMA courses, AARs).

rto_tiers: RTO priority ratings with maximum allowed recovery times: < 4 hours, < 24 hours, < 1 week; each department assigns an RTO to every essential function in its continuity plan
vital_records_approach: Business-unit continuity plan template includes a Vital Records table (name, vendor, primary location, alternate location) plus Publications/Programs-and-Software tables with primary/alternate locations
tte_cadence: Multi-Year Training and Exercise Plan (MYTEP, Appendix E) integrating EM and continuity programs; training in workshops/seminars/role-specific formats and exercises of ranging size/type; after-action reports and a Corrective Action Program; business units exercise plans at least annually
reconstitution_treatment: Framed as the recurring objective of 'return to normal university operations'; Continuity Plan defined as procedures to 'respond, recover, resume, and restore university essential functions to a predefined level'; no distinct reconstitution process/section
academic_continuity_treatment: Program explicitly encompasses 'academic, business, and information technology (IT) continuity of operations plans'; teaching/instruction and research are named essential functions; academic units (all colleges, Academic Senate, Pollak Library) are designated business units that must hold COOPs
it_continuity_treatment: Separate CSUF Information Technology Disaster Recovery Plan (ITDR) under the Division of IT covers recovery of technology facilities/platforms; the Campus Continuity Coordinator aligns DRPs and continuity plans and ensures RTOs/RPOs are communicated to IT with gap remediation
interdependencies_treatment: BIA determines 'essential functions, respective business units, and interdependencies'; business-unit template has a dedicated Interdependencies section; coordination with other CSU campuses and local/state/federal agencies

unusual_decisions.decision: Continuity authority is delegated from the President to the Chief of Police as Campus Continuity Coordinator, with the Emergency Management & Continuity Coordinator as backup and an EM&C Assistant as tertiary backup
unusual_decisions.quote: The President delegates authority to the Chief of Police to prioritize the development, implementation and maintenance of a Cal State Fullerton Continuity Program to ensure compliance with the CSU System Continuity Program.

unusual_decisions.decision: Explicitly aligns the program's guiding principles to the university's six Guiding Principles of Social Justice
unusual_decisions.quote: Social Justice: The CP intentionally aligns with CSUF's six Guiding Principles of Social Justice, including: Enhance Equitable Learning; Expand Intentional Outreach; Amplify Hispanic Serving Institution Designation & Radiate an Identity of Inclusion; Building a Professorial-to-Leadership Pipeline; Recognize History and Catalyze Change; and Create an Antiracist Community.

unusual_decisions.decision: Treats academic, business, and IT continuity as three coordinated COOP streams feeding one campus CONOPS
unusual_decisions.quote: Each individual essential function listed on page 4 has responsibility over their individual plans that feed into the overall CSUF Continuity Concept of Operations Plan (CONOPS), which is maintained by the OEMC.

analyst_notes: Read all 82 pages. CSUF's 2026 Continuity Program is a polished FCD-element/program-level document with a President-signed delegation of authority (12/11/2025), a continuity CONOPS, a hazard/risk assessment (Table B.1), a BIA with three RTO tiers, an essential-functions/business-unit inventory (34 units), a crisis communication plan, a vital-records and interdependencies business-unit template, a separate IT-DR plan, and a MYTEP with AAR/Corrective Action Program. The two clear gaps are orders of succession for leadership positions (only continuity-coordinator backups are named; the word 'succession' never appears) and devolution.

essential_functions: Information Technology (network & data, telephone, IT services)
Facilities and Campus Services (transportation, facilities management)
Dean of Students (student & community crisis support)
rto_tiers: Essential services prioritized; Emergency Support Functions carry 'immediate recovery time objectives'; activation within 12 hours of a significant disruption, sustained up to 30 days; academic-suspension timing rules (first three weeks = semester lost; last three weeks = may be completed)
succession_depth: University Order of Succession three deep (President -> Provost -> Secretary of the Corporation -> CFO -> Dean of Weill Medical College), Table 6.1, defined by University bylaws
vital_records_approach: Section H Vital Files, Records, and Databases with a Vital Records Maintenance Strategy table (develop a process, identify vital records via the C-COOP tool, review protection needs); content not media determines criticality; CIT network security plan supports secure records
tte_cadence: Not established as a program; TT&E appears only in the COOP definition ('validate the capability through tests, training, and exercises') and as an after-action review/corrective action step during reconstitution; no training plan, exercise types, or recurring cadence
devolution_treatment: Not addressed (succession is provided but no devolution of essential functions to a separate unit/site)
reconstitution_treatment: Dedicated 'Reconstitution and Recovery' section: demobilize resources, supervise orderly return to normal/temporary/new facility, verify systems, conduct an after-action review and corrective/remedial action plan
academic_continuity_treatment: Detailed Academic Continuity section (alternative course delivery/distance learning, semester-timing rules for suspension, equitable treatment of students lacking technology) plus a separate Research Operations Continuity section (space/equipment documentation, inter-college sharing agreements, data/organism backup)
it_continuity_treatment: Section I Critical Business Applications and Technology Resources: connectivity and system access required at the continuity location, servers backed up frequently at more than one location, CIT maintains a disaster continuity and contingency plan for central service applications
interdependencies_treatment: Establishes communication with supporting and supported organizations and stakeholders; inter-college agreements to share space; coordination with CIT, Office of Space Planning, Registrar; C-COOP tool maps unit resource needs

unusual_decisions.decision: Provides a university-wide web-based continuity-planning tool (C-COOP) as the central repository for unit essential services, priorities, personnel/resource needs, and vital-records mapping
unusual_decisions.quote: To facilitate alignment with continuity of operations guiding principles, the University provides a robust, web-based software tool called C-COOP (www.coop.ehs.cornell.edu).

unusual_decisions.decision: Codifies academic-calendar decision rules for how much of a term is lost based on when instruction is suspended
unusual_decisions.quote: As a very general presumption, if the suspension of instruction occurs within the first three weeks of a term, the semester will be considered to be lost and will need to be repeated in its entirety. If the suspension of instruction occurs in the last three weeks of the term, the semester may be considered to be completed, subject to course-specific exceptions.

unusual_decisions.decision: Treats extraordinary preparation costs as a unit responsibility funded from unit reserves, with central funding only via the annual budget process
unusual_decisions.quote: Such costs will generally be considered a financial responsibility of the operating unit, to be funded out of current available resources including unit reserves... funding may be requested as part of the annual budget process to the university Operating Plans Committee.

analyst_notes: Read all 18 pages. Cornell's COOP v3.0 is an exemplary FCD-element plan: a concept of operations, NIMS/National Disaster Recovery Framework alignment with a formal Authorities and References appendix (NFPA 1600, EMAP, FEMA CPG 101/201, SUNY requirements) and a Policy 8.3 promulgation statement, essential services (ESF table), succession (Table 6.1, three deep), delegations of authority, continuity locations, vital records, financial/budget guidance, IT continuity (CIT), and strong academic and research continuity. The two clear gaps are devolution (succession is not devolution) and a structured TT&E program (tests/training/exercises are only named in the definition and an after-action step, with no training plan, exercise types, or cadence).

essential_functions: EF#1 University Leadership (President, Provost, VP Administration & Finance) - RTO 4 hours
EF#2 Public Safety - continuous (no recovery time)
EF#3 Communications (alert notification, police dispatch, executive/media) - tools RTO 30 min / content RTO 4 hours
EF#4 Basic Services (food, emergency maintenance, access control, transportation) - RTO 6-12 hours
EF#5 Fiscal Operations and Functional Processes (payroll, purchasing, contracts, critical data) - RTO 3 hours
EF#6 Academic Continuity (instruction, research, space, pivot to virtual) - RTO 3 days, some research continuous
rto_tiers: Per-Mission-Essential-Function recovery times (30 minutes to 3 days); operational capability within 12 hours of activation; academic classes reinstated within 2 weeks; sustained operations up to 30 days
succession_depth: At least three positions deep where possible; orders-of-succession tables for seven offices (President, Academic Affairs, Administration & Finance, Advancement & University Relations, Enrollment Management, Human Resources, Student Success & Student Life), described by position/title and including devolution counterparts
vital_records_approach: Extensive Essential Records section keyed to Texas Government Code 441.180 'vital State record'; records digitized and accessible virtually, IT responsible for security/recovery, hard copies scanned to network/portable drive; access available at continuity facility within 12 hours; BIA and insurance policies designated essential records
tte_cadence: Dedicated Test, Training, Exercises section; colleges/divisions/departments train on their COOP and exercise it annually, submitting documentation to the Continuity Coordinator by April 1; After-Action Review, Corrective Action Plan, and remedial action plan; successors and continuity personnel participate
devolution_treatment: Dedicated Devolution of Control and Direction section: transfers statutory authority and responsibility for essential functions from UHD's primary staff/facilities to a University of Houston System campus's employees/facilities if leadership/staff are unavailable, sustained for an extended period
reconstitution_treatment: Phase 4 Reconstitution in the CONOPS plus a dedicated Reconstitution section; surviving/replacement personnel resume normal operations; redeployment plans for return to the primary facility
academic_continuity_treatment: Academic Continuity is Mission Essential Function #6 (maintain instruction, research, and space; transition to a virtual environment when campus is untenable; RTO 3 days, some research continuous); plan targets reinstating classes within 2 weeks
it_continuity_treatment: Supplemented by an extensive IT COOP and Disaster Recovery Plan maintained by the AVP of IT (an essential record); IT responsible for security/recovery of vital state records in digital format; computer systems backed up regularly with access available at the Emergency Relocation Site
interdependencies_treatment: Departments report dependencies and business processes via the BIA process; assumptions detail reliance on UH System resources, utilities, telecom, financial/public-health/security/transportation services; coordination with HDMD, City of Houston, Harris County

unusual_decisions.decision: Uses reciprocal University of Houston System campuses as the Emergency Relocation Site and as the devolution destination, providing classrooms/labs/library/computing within 12 hours
unusual_decisions.quote: If UHD is unable to deploy an ERG to an ERS due to loss of personnel, UHD will devolve to one of the University of Houston System campuses.

unusual_decisions.decision: Sets distinct recovery-time objectives for communications tools versus communications content
unusual_decisions.quote: The recovery time objective for the tools needed to communicate is 30 minutes while the recovery time objective for the content needed to communicate is four (4) hours.

unusual_decisions.decision: Designates the key personnel relocated under the plan as a named Emergency Relocation Group operating from an Emergency Relocation Site or virtual environment
unusual_decisions.quote: Key personnel relocated under this plan are collectively known as the UHD Emergency Relocation Group (ERG). Upon plan activation, these members may move to a virtual environment or deploy to a continuity facility and establish operational capability to perform essential functions within 12 hours from the time of plan activation up to a 30-day period or until normal operations can be resumed.

analyst_notes: NOTE: the supplied 2023 URL returned HTTP 404; I coded UHD's current 2025-2026 COOP (same institution, same full-coop document type) fetched from uhd.edu (2025-uhd-coop-campus.pdf). Read all 55 pages. This is a complete FCD-element COOP: signed promulgation statement, four-phase CONOPS (Readiness/Activation/Operations/Reconstitution), six Mission Essential Functions with explicit RTOs, three-deep succession tables across seven offices, delegations of authority with triggers/limits, a robust Essential Records section, alternate ERS facilities plus telework, interoperable communications, a dedicated devolution section, a budgeting & acquisition section, a TT&E program with AAR/CAP, and citations to FEMA's Continuity Guidance Circular, PPD-40/NSPD-51 National Continuity Policy, and Texas statutes. All 22 components present.

essential_functions: UT Police (building access controls, crowd control)
Office of Public Affairs Communications Team
Environmental Health & Safety and Risk Management (sanitation, hazardous waste, insurance assessments)
Center for Laboratory Animal Medicine and Care (animal care)
Auxiliary Enterprises (housing, parking, mail, shuttle, dining, bookstore)
rto_tiers: IT Disaster Recovery RTO under 48 hours for ~30 critical systems, RPO ranging from minutes to hours; non-critical systems vary with some rebuilds up to 30 days; plan governs the 30-day post-event window
vital_records_approach: Data protection via network/offsite backups and a 'Protection of Data' worksheet (network-drive saves, physically separate backups, recovery of locally retained data); asset documentation for insurance recovery; IT-DRP replicates critical systems to secondary datacenters (Guhn Rd, Arlington) or offsite backup
tte_cadence: IT-DR maintenance schedule lists annual 'Critical system DR/end-user testing' and 'Update testing schedule' tasks with scheduled reviews; no broader BCP exercise program (tabletop/functional), training program, or after-action/improvement cycle for the BCP itself
reconstitution_treatment: Not addressed as a distinct return-to-normal process; plan scope is limited to the ~30-day post-event recovery window
academic_continuity_treatment: Limited to academic-support continuity: Registrar's Office tracks courses/grades for fulfillment of academic requirements and processes new applicants; Canvas LMS and academic systems are in the IT-DRP; no instructional pivot-to-remote plan
it_continuity_treatment: Detailed standalone IT Disaster Recovery Plan for ~30 critical systems (PeopleSoft FMS/HRMS, COUPA, Epic EHR, Canvas) using ASP-contracted DR, secondary-datacenter replication, or offsite backup/rebuild; RTO under 48h, RPO minutes-to-hours; dedicated IT Disaster Recovery Team feeds status to Public Affairs
interdependencies_treatment: Operational entities explicitly rely on the eleven institutional infrastructure services; functional-process tables show affected systems/impacts; critical-vendor and peer-to-peer (in/out of region) support identified in worksheets; financial transactions dependent on IT-infrastructure status

unusual_decisions.decision: Scopes the entire plan to the 30-day post-event window, justified by lessons from Tropical Storm Allison
unusual_decisions.quote: Based upon the experience with Tropical Storm Allison, 30 days post event is the most critical timeframe with regard to immediate recovery decisions, resource procurement, and the establishment of temporary means and locations of essential functions.

unusual_decisions.decision: Builds the support-services BCP as paper-form 'Disaster Recovery Mode' workflows for procurement, cash, HR, payroll and asset management to run when information systems are down
unusual_decisions.quote: In the absence of institution-wide information system access, the tables following on pages 8-12 provide an overview of the processes related to procurement, capital asset management, cash management, human resources/payroll, and Auxiliary Enterprises parking.

unusual_decisions.decision: Designates a color-coded (green/yellow/red) building-and-operational status updated twice daily during impairment
unusual_decisions.quote: This form is to be completed by the authorized facility/operations coordinator while a facility remains in a yellow or red status and/or operations are impaired.

analyst_notes: Read all 73 pages. This is UTHealth Houston's 'Business Continuity Plan - Infrastructure (Support Services),' a March 2025 companion to the Emergency Management Plan and IT Disaster Recovery Plan; the latter half (Appendices I-III) is operational flowcharts, disaster-preparedness/building-status worksheets, and manual finance/HR/equipment forms (the form fields seen on download). It is strong on essential infrastructure functions, business-process analysis, IT-DR with RTO/RPO, interdependencies, communications, vital-data protection, version control, and an IT testing schedule. As a deliberately narrow support-services companion document it omits a signed promulgation, a continuity CONOPS, recognized continuity-doctrine citations (only a worksheet 'adapted from ready.gov'), a threat/hazard assessment (deferred to the EMP), budgeting, orders of succession, delegations of authority, devolution, and a reconstitution/return-to-normal process.

essential_functions: plus a tiered hierarchy of State Essential Functions, Institutional Essential Functions, Primary Mission Essential Functions, Mission Essential Functions, Essential Supporting Activities, and Mission Enhancing Activities defined per College/Division/School/Office
rto_tiers: Defines Max Tolerable Downtime (MTD), Recovery Time Objective (RTO), and Recovery Point Objective (RPO) for each MEF; PMEFs must remain continuous or resume within 12 hours and be maintained up to 30 days or until normal operations resume; BIA scores impact 0 (none) to 5 (severe)
vital_records_approach: BPA Step 9 requires identifying 'standard operating procedures, essential records, and reference materials'; MEF data sheet documents required records; no central vital-records protection/storage program (responsibility pushed to departmental plans)
tte_cadence: Continuity Coordinators attend initial and annual continuity training; Deans/VPs ensure plans are 'developed, coordinated, exercised, and capable of being implemented'; emergency action procedures activated during continuity exercises; BIA review/validation at least every two years; no exercise-type structure or formal after-action/improvement cycle
reconstitution_treatment: Not addressed as a distinct process; 'until normal operations resume' is a stated endpoint only
academic_continuity_treatment: Teaching & Learning (UEF 5), Student Opportunity & Success (UEF 4), and Research (UEF 2) are University Essential Functions; framework relies on remote work, noting COVID-19 showed many MEFs can continue virtually while some have mandatory in-person components
it_continuity_treatment: BPA Step 7 requires identifying communications/information-system requirements, data-access needs, system dependencies and interfaces; RPO defined; but no IT disaster-recovery program, backup strategy, or alternate-processing capability is established (identification only)
interdependencies_treatment: Strong: work-function inventory links interdependencies, BPA Step 4 identifies dependencies/interdependencies (partners, points of contact, support type, timelines), and MEFs are prioritized when they support multiple PMEFs across Colleges/Divisions

unusual_decisions.decision: Builds a seven-level essential-functions hierarchy (State -> Institutional -> University -> Primary Mission -> Mission -> Essential Supporting Activities -> Mission Enhancing Activities) cascading from State of Maryland and USM requirements down to office level
unusual_decisions.quote: Essential functions are the building blocks of continuity... Each level us supported by the functions below, and dependent on the functions above.

unusual_decisions.decision: Maps the five University Essential Functions directly onto the five pillars of the UMBC Strategic Plan
unusual_decisions.quote: UMBC has five UEFs that mirror the five strategic pillars detailed in the UMBC Strategic Plan and form the basis for all our continuity decisions.

unusual_decisions.decision: Deliberately designs the framework for non-expert staff for whom continuity is a collateral duty, generating data sheets automatically from online BPA/BIA worksheets
unusual_decisions.quote: simplified planning and training will help minimize confusion and enable effective decision-making in a time of crisis.

analyst_notes: Read all 30 pages. NOTE: the supplied 2025-UMB-COOP-Plan.pdf URL was a different institution (University of Maryland, Baltimore) and returned HTTP 404; this object codes the document actually mapped to the umbc.edu police-site URL given, UMBC's EOP Annex 3 COOP (Rev. May 2026), a framework/methodology plan. It is a strategic-level framework with a signed approval (Chief of Police), USM VI-13.00 / State of Maryland MDEM COOP-guide authority, an integrated-response concept of operations, a rigorous four-phase planning process (Identify MEFs -> BPA -> BIA -> Prioritize/Document), MTD/RTO/RPO, an HVRA-based threat assessment, deep interdependency mapping, budget/resource and essential-records identification, continuity personnel (CWG/EMAG), activation triggers, and academic continuity via remote work. Because it is a planning framework that pushes execution to departmental DEAPs, it omits orders of succession, formal delegations of authority, a resilient continuity-communications capability, devolution, a reconstitution process, and an IT disaster-recovery program (IT needs are identified per-MEF but no DR/backup program is established).

rto_tiers: Generic guidance only ('full operational capability for essential functions no later than 12 hours after activation... sustain operations for up to 30 days, 60 days during a Pandemic'); no institution-specific RTO/RPO/MTD
vital_records_approach: Names 'Essential Records' as one of the 10 elements but provides no TTU-System vital-records identification, protection, or access provisions
tte_cadence: Advocates TT&E generically as Program Phase 5 and a planning consideration ('regularly scheduled testing, training & exercising'); no TTU-System cadence, exercise types, or after-action process
devolution_treatment: Named as element 8 ('Devolution of control') with no TTU-System provisions
reconstitution_treatment: Named as element 9 and Phase 7 ('Implementation of reconstitution and devolution if necessary') with no TTU-System provisions
academic_continuity_treatment: Not addressed beyond listing Provost/CAO as example COOP-team contributors
interdependencies_treatment: Not addressed (BPA named as a Phase-2 activity but no dependency mapping)
analyst_notes: Read all 15 slides. This artifact is not an operational continuity plan: it is a 2017 PowerPoint 'Continuity of Operations (COOP) Program Overview' by Kyle Gunn, Assistant Director, Office of Risk Management, that teaches what a COOP is. It enumerates the 10 FEMA/FCD continuity elements and a 7-phase program-development model and gives a Wisconsin-fire activation example, but contains no Texas Tech University System-specific provisions for any element. I therefore code only the program concept of operations (the lifecycle/program model) as present and list the remaining 21 components as missing because they are described generically rather than established for the institution. Data-quality flag: the source document_type ('full-coop') does not match this awareness/training deck.

essential_functions: University Enterprises and Events (Campus Dining, St. Michael's Laundry, Cedar Grove)
rto_tiers: Template requires setting 'Minimum operational levels and recovery time frames' and a 'Maximum Time to go without' the essential function; no institution-wide tier scheme
tte_cadence: Each required area reviews its plan and exercises it at least annually (tabletop or part of the larger University annual drill); training of affected personnel is documented; RMS coordinates the University's annual drill and reports gaps
reconstitution_treatment: Framed as the program's overall purpose ('managing the University's return to an operational status after an incident') but no reconstitution process is described
academic_continuity_treatment: Guiding principle of 'Continuity of student learning, teaching and research'; ND Research (Provost) is a required-plan department; no instructional pivot or academic-calendar provisions
it_continuity_treatment: Office of Information Technologies is on the required-plan list and the template asks units to list 'IT Infrastructure Requirements,' but no IT disaster-recovery program, backups, or alternate processing is established
interdependencies_treatment: Template requires documenting dependencies (power, IT, people), suppliers/contractors with MOUs or open POs, and an optional table of internal/external notification and 'response to get infrastructure back online'

unusual_decisions.decision: Directs that any existing policy conflicting with disruption needs be amended rather than worked around
unusual_decisions.quote: Amending existing policies that require provisions for specific recovery situations.

unusual_decisions.decision: Provides a worked template example (campus dining producing 7,500 meals/day, fire protection) showing infrastructure, dependencies, max time without, and get-back-online actions
unusual_decisions.quote: Produce at least 7,500 meals per day ... 2 days ... Buy food from another company - list vendor

unusual_decisions.decision: Centralizes storage of all departmental plans with Risk Management and Safety while units also retain their own copies
unusual_decisions.quote: Risk Management and Safety shall maintain copies of the business continuity plans for each division and department required to maintain/develop one.

analyst_notes: Read all 7 pages. Notre Dame's Business Continuity Program (SAFE028, August 2021) is a short framework/policy document plus a BCP template: it names owners and an approval date, requires 13 listed departments to build/maintain/test plans annually, defines mission-critical functions, and supplies a template covering essential functions, dependencies/suppliers (MOUs), recovery time frames, a communications plan, and outsource options. It deliberately focuses 'on critical functions rather than incident causation,' so it omits a CONOPS, recognized continuity-doctrine citations, a threat/risk assessment, succession, delegations of authority, a vital-records program, activation triggers, devolution, a reconstitution process, and an IT disaster-recovery program.

essential_functions: Critical functions defined per unit via BIA (no enumerated university list); examples reference Education and Human Ecology, Office of the CIO, Optometry, Student Life; functional domains include teaching, learning, research, outreach and engagement, student life, patient care, and supporting business operations
rto_tiers: Units provide RTOs and RPOs annually for the systems/applications they own; BIA gives guidelines on tolerable down time; no fixed tier scheme in the policy
tte_cadence: Units conduct BCP testing exercises every two years, type chosen by the BCM Office and unit (live/simulation, real-world, review/discussion, scenario/tabletop); annual BCP review/approval; BIA every two years; risk assessment every three years; performance scorecard, continuous improvement, lessons learned
reconstitution_treatment: BCRs develop procedures for the 'recovery phases' and the program aims to 'restore the university community back to normal in the case of a disruption'
academic_continuity_treatment: Policy commits to 'ensuring the continuity of teaching, learning, research, outreach and engagement, student life experiences, patient care experiences, business operations, and the critical functions that support them'; academic college clusters represented on the steering committee
it_continuity_treatment: BCR continuity strategies for loss of applications/systems (alternative storage, back-up of systems, manual workaround); Enterprise Security Disaster Recovery sits on the BCM Steering Committee; external references to ISO 27031 IT-DR alignment with ISO 22301
interdependencies_treatment: BCM Office provides an annual 'gap report of operational need versus systems and applications (dependencies) capability'; BCR strategies address supplier/supply-chain loss; RTOs/RPOs communicated across units

unusual_decisions.decision: Centralizes BCM in a dedicated Business Continuity Management Office in Business and Finance with a cross-cluster Steering Committee reporting to the Risk Management Committee and Board of Trustees
unusual_decisions.quote: The Business Continuity Management Office in the Office of Business and Finance is responsible for directing business continuity management (BCM) for the university and units.

unusual_decisions.decision: Aligns the program explicitly with recognized BCM standards and external bodies
unusual_decisions.quote: To set requirements for BCM, maintain continued availability of critical functions, and align university practices with BCM industry standards.

unusual_decisions.decision: Treats noncompliance as an enforceable policy matter with tracked exceptions and possible corrective action up to termination
unusual_decisions.quote: The university may enforce corrective action, up to and including termination, in accordance with applicable policies or rules.

analyst_notes: Read all 5 pages. Ohio State's Business Continuity Management University Policy (issued 2020, edited 2023 and 2025) is a strong governance framework: a promulgated university policy with a responsible office, explicit alignment to ISO 22301/ISO 27031 and BCI/DRII, critical-function/BIA/risk-assessment requirements, RTO/RPO collection, a BCM Office and Steering Committee, biennial multi-type testing exercises with a performance scorecard and continuous improvement, alternate-site and IT-recovery strategies, and academic/patient-care continuity. As a policy it does not establish orders of succession, formal delegations of authority, a resilient continuity-communications capability, a vital-records program, activation triggers/decision authority, or devolution.

essential_functions: Essential business functions identified and prioritized per unit via the BIA (no enumerated university-wide list); units identify, document, and prioritize their key business functions
rto_tiers: BIA establishes 'maximum allowable downtime and recovery time objectives (RTOs) for each function' and 'acceptable level of losses and recovery point objectives (RPOs)'; no fixed tier scheme
tte_cadence: Annual BCP review and annual training/refresh (new staff trained annually, confirmed in a Quality Assurance Review); every two years each unit conducts a tabletop exercise/drill with a documented, retained exercise assessment
reconstitution_treatment: Stated as a BCP aim ('efficiently return to normal operations') but no reconstitution process described
academic_continuity_treatment: Requires faculty inclusion per the Faculty Handbook and applies to faculty/academic officers, but no instructional-continuity, pivot-to-remote, or academic-calendar provisions
it_continuity_treatment: Explicitly out of scope: disaster recovery plans developed by Information Systems and Facility Operations are excluded from this policy
interdependencies_treatment: BIA requires units to 'identify, document, and communicate interdependencies with other units or among functions within a unit' and to map critical paths/dependencies for restoring functions

unusual_decisions.decision: Explicitly carves IT disaster recovery, emergency response, and crisis communications out of the BCM policy's scope
unusual_decisions.quote: Out of the scope of this policy are: Emergency response plans (defined by the Emergency Procedures and Communications Policy), Crisis management and crisis communications plans, and Disaster recovery plans (developed by Information Systems and Facility Operations).

unusual_decisions.decision: Mandates a structured six-step BCM process (BIA, risk assessment, risk-reduction strategies, BCP development, training, biennial evaluation) for every unit
unusual_decisions.quote: Units must complete a six-step process and annually review each step to ensure currency of plans and the unit's ability to execute the plans.

unusual_decisions.decision: Stores confidential unit BCPs in access-controlled repositories (DePaul Knowledgebase wiki or OneDrive) chosen by the unit
unusual_decisions.quote: Since BCPs may contain information about DePaul's internal business processes that is confidential in nature the repository must also have access control.

analyst_notes: Read all 6 pages. DePaul's Business Continuity Management Policy (effective 2021, originated 2017) is a unit-level program/policy: a responsible-officer-owned policy mandating a six-step process per unit (BIA with RTO/RPO/MTD and interdependencies, risk assessment, risk-reduction strategies, a BCP that includes succession plans and facility alternatives, annual training, biennial tabletop exercises), with access-controlled plan repositories and an annual review cycle. It is deliberately subservient to the Emergency Procedures policy and EOP, and it expressly excludes IT disaster recovery and crisis communications, so it omits a CONOPS, recognized continuity-doctrine citations, budgeting, delegations of authority, resilient continuity communications, a vital-records program, activation triggers, devolution, a reconstitution process, IT-DR, and instructional continuity. Orders of succession are present because the required BCP must include 'succession plans for individuals in leadership role or with specialized expertise.'
